links.text: Salk Institute for Biological Studies — Wikipedia

summary: Louis Kahn (Louis Isadore Kahn, born Itze-Leib Schmuilowsky) was an Estonian-American architect based in Philadelphia whose work blended Modernist geometry with monumental materials. His most celebrated buildings include the Salk Institute in La Jolla, the National Parliament House of Bangladesh in Dhaka, the Kimbell Art Museum in Fort Worth, the Phillips Exeter Academy Library, and the Richards Medical Research Laboratories at Penn. He died alone in a restroom at Penn Station and was the subject of the documentary My Architect by his son Nathaniel.
comprehensive_summary:
Louis Kahn (1901–1974) was an Estonian-American architect based in Philadelphia who developed one of the twentieth century's most distinctive architectural philosophies, centered on the opposition between 'served' and 'servant' spaces, an obsession with natural light, and the expressive use of brick and concrete. He famously told his students to 'talk to brick,' envisioning the material as saying 'I like an arch.' Wendy Lesser's biography of him is titled You Say to Brick.

The Salk Institute for Biological Studies in La Jolla, California (completed 1965) is perhaps his most celebrated work. Two streams of water separate three rows of buildings made of teak and varieties of concrete, framing a view of the Pacific Ocean along a plaza. Each office space has a view of the Pacific. The design was a collaboration with engineer August E. Komendant.

The Kimbell Art Museum in Fort Worth, Texas consists of sixteen parallel cycloid (barrel vault) shapes with light entering through skylights at the apex of each vault. Michelangelo's first painting — The Torment of Saint Anthony — is a highlight of the collection. A later expansion was designed by Renzo Piano.

The National Parliament House (Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban) in Dhaka, Bangladesh was Kahn's last project. Eight peripheral blocks surround a central octagonal block, and the building is surrounded by a symbolic artificial lake. Multistory cutouts in a repeated pattern of triangle on top of square next to circle mark the outer walls. It was designed in close collaboration with Bangladeshi architects.

The Phillips Exeter Academy Library is designed in three concentric square rings: the outer ring is load-bearing red brick; the innermost ring is a central atrium with four large circular holes in its concrete walls revealing different floors of the middle ring. A large X-shaped beam of concrete spreads light through it.

The Richards Medical Research Laboratories at the University of Pennsylvania exemplify his 'served' and 'servant' spaces theory, grouping stairwells and elevators in large concrete brick-clad shafts separate from the lab spaces. His Trenton Bath House was an earlier application of this theory. The Indian Institute of Management in Ahmedabad also used red brick for its facade.

Kahn designed an early free art gallery at Yale University with a triangular coffered concrete ceiling. He also designed the Center for British Art at Yale using an innovative mix of concrete and light-colored wood. He designed the First Unitarian Church in Rochester, NY, organized around the shape of a question mark. He collaborated with Isamu Noguchi on an unrealized playground design. His body was not identified for two days after he died alone in a men's room at Penn Station in 1974. His son Nathaniel directed the Oscar-winning documentary My Architect about his life.

works.name: Salk Institute for Biological Studies
works.indicator: La Jolla; teak and concrete; water channel between buildings; each office has Pacific view; citrus grove courtyard
works.description: The Salk Institute in La Jolla, California features two streams of water separating three rows of buildings made of teak and varieties of concrete, framing a Pacific Ocean view. Each office has a view of the Pacific. The design was made in collaboration with engineer August E. Komendant. A citrus grove courtyard is also included.

works.name: National Parliament House, Bangladesh
works.indicator: Dhaka, Bangladesh; eight peripheral blocks around central octagonal block; artificial lake; triangle-square-circle cutout pattern; Kahn's last project
works.description: The Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban (National Parliament House) in Dhaka is Kahn's last project. Eight peripheral blocks surround a central octagonal block. The building is surrounded by a symbolic artificial lake and its outer walls feature a repeated cutout pattern of triangle on top of square next to circle. Assembly chamber lit by large semi-circle-shaped windows.

works.name: Yale University Art Gallery
works.indicator: Triangular coffered concrete ceiling; early commission; free to public
works.description: Kahn's Yale University Art Gallery features a triangular coffered concrete ceiling. It was one of his earliest commissions and was free to the public. He also designed the Yale Center for British Art with an innovative mix of concrete and light-colored wood.

clues.text: Triangular coffered concrete ceiling; free to the public
One of his earliest commissions at Yale University
Yale Center for British Art uses concrete and light-colored wood

works.name: Biographical and Other Works
works.indicator: Died alone in men's room at Penn Station; son Nathaniel directed Oscar-winning documentary My Architect; told students to 'talk to brick'; Indian Institute of Management Ahmedabad (red brick); First Unitarian Church Rochester (shape of a question mark)
works.description: Kahn died alone in a men's room at Penn Station in 1974; his body took two days to identify. His son Nathaniel directed the Oscar-winning documentary My Architect about him. Kahn famously told students to 'talk to brick.' His biography by Wendy Lesser is titled You Say to Brick. He used red brick for the Indian Institute of Management in Ahmedabad. He designed the First Unitarian Church in Rochester, NY around the shape of a question mark. He collaborated with Isamu Noguchi on an unrealized playground design. He taught Robert Venturi. His long-uncredited collaborator was Anne Tyng.

Long-uncredited collaborator Anne Tyng
